title: Indie folk

* [Alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock")[[1]](#cite_note-about.com-1) * [folk](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") * [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock")

(also called **alternative folk**) is an [alternative](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") genre of music that arose in the 1990s among musicians from [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") scenes influenced by [folk music](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Alternative genre of folk music

| Indie folk | |
| --- | --- |
| Other names | Alternative folk |
| Stylistic origins | * [Alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock")[[1]](#cite_note-about.com-1) * [folk](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") * [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") |
| Cultural origins | 1990s |
| Subgenres | |
| Stomp and holler | |
| Regional scenes | |
| [Senja (genre)](/wiki/Senja_(genre) "Senja (genre)") | |
| Other topics | |
| * [Acoustic rock](/wiki/Acoustic_rock "Acoustic rock") * [folk pop](/wiki/Folk_pop "Folk pop") * [folk punk](/wiki/Folk_punk "Folk punk") * [folk rock](/wiki/Folk_rock "Folk rock") | |

**Indie folk** (also called **alternative folk**) is an [alternative](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") genre of music that arose in the 1990s among musicians from [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") scenes influenced by [folk music](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music").

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_folk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

The staff of *[Paste Magazine](/wiki/Paste_(magazine) "Paste (magazine)")* said in 2020: "No music genre is particularly easy to define, but “indie folk” is about as nebulous as they come."[[2]](#cite_note-2) Indie folk hybridizes the [acoustic guitar](/wiki/Acoustic_guitar "Acoustic guitar") melodies of [traditional folk music](/wiki/Folk_music#Traditional_folk_music "Folk music") with contemporary instrumentation. The lyrical style commonly includes raw emotional experiences, social commentary and an introspective lens.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The genre blends the ethos and experimental nature of indie music with the storytelling of folk music. Instruments frequently used in the genre include guitars, banjos, mandolins, and ukuleles.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

## Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_folk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Influences")]

### 1960s–1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_folk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1960s–1970s")]

[John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") and [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") have been cited as influences for indie folk, with [Phoebe Bridgers](/wiki/Phoebe_Bridgers "Phoebe Bridgers") stating, "I love John Lennon,” she continues “Easily best Beatle. He’s been such an icon for so many people who are my heroes, like [Elliott Smith](/wiki/Elliott_Smith "Elliott Smith") and [Daniel Johnston](/wiki/Daniel_Johnston "Daniel Johnston")".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Additionally, [The Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground") influenced the genre with their raw and [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music"), which had a profound impact on all alternative and indie genres.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Other Influences include [Elvis Costello](/wiki/Elvis_Costello "Elvis Costello"), [Leonard Cohen](/wiki/Leonard_Cohen "Leonard Cohen"), [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Simon and Garfunkel](/wiki/Simon_%26_Garfunkel "Simon & Garfunkel"), [The Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds"), [Nico](/wiki/Nico "Nico"), and [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_folk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

The genre has its earliest origins in 1990s folk artists who displayed alternative rock influences in their music, such as [Ani DiFranco](/wiki/Ani_DiFranco "Ani DiFranco") and [Dan Bern](/wiki/Dan_Bern "Dan Bern"), and acoustic artists such as Elliott Smith and [Will Oldham](/wiki/Will_Oldham "Will Oldham").[[1]](#cite_note-about.com-1)[[8]](#cite_note-AllMusic-8) In the following decade, labels such as [Saddle Creek](/wiki/Saddle_Creek_Records "Saddle Creek Records"), [Barsuk](/wiki/Barsuk_Records "Barsuk Records"), Ramseur, and [Sub Pop](/wiki/Sub_Pop "Sub Pop") helped to provide support to indie folk,[[8]](#cite_note-AllMusic-8) with artists such as [Fleet Foxes](/wiki/Fleet_Foxes "Fleet Foxes") breaking into the [pop charts](/wiki/Record_chart "Record chart") with albums such as *[Helplessness Blues](/wiki/Helplessness_Blues "Helplessness Blues")*.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Fleetfoxes08.jpg/250px-Fleetfoxes08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fleetfoxes08.jpg)

Fleet Foxes

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), artists such as [Ben Howard](/wiki/Ben_Howard "Ben Howard")[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) and [Mumford & Sons](/wiki/Mumford_%26_Sons "Mumford & Sons")[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-auto-20) emerged, with the latter band promoting the music style through their Gentlemen of the Road touring festivals.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) The success of acts like Mumford & Sons led some music journalists like [Popjustice's](/wiki/Popjustice "Popjustice") [Peter Robinson](/wiki/Peter_Robinson_(journalist) "Peter Robinson (journalist)") labelling this new British music scene as The New Boring or Beige Pop.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[20]](#cite_note-auto-20) A decade later, the terms "stomp and holler," "stomp clap hey," and "hoedown pop" were coined to refer retrospectively, sometimes with endearment, to such bands.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, most of the time, "stomp clap hey" and similar terms are used in a derogatory way, with the release of [Kyle Gordon](/wiki/Kyle_Gordon "Kyle Gordon")'s "We Will Never Die" (recorded under the pseudonym of Kody Redwing and the Broken Hearts) popularizing hatred of 2010s indie folk.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Phoebe_Bridgers_%2842690581094%29.jpg/250px-Phoebe_Bridgers_%2842690581094%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phoebe_Bridgers_(42690581094).jpg)

Phoebe Bridgers

In the late 2010s and in the 2020s, artists such as [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift"), [Phoebe Bridgers](/wiki/Phoebe_Bridgers "Phoebe Bridgers"), and [Julien Baker](/wiki/Julien_Baker "Julien Baker") revived interest in the genre with Swift's *[Folklore](/wiki/Folklore_(Taylor_Swift_album) "Folklore (Taylor Swift album)")* and *[Evermore](/wiki/Evermore "Evermore"),* as well as Bridgers' *[Punisher](/wiki/Punisher_(album) "Punisher (album)")* and Baker's *[Sprained Ankle](/wiki/Sprained_Ankle_(album) "Sprained Ankle (album)")*, *[Turn Out the Lights](/wiki/Turn_Out_the_Lights_(album) "Turn Out the Lights (album)")*, and *[Little Oblivions](/wiki/Little_Oblivions "Little Oblivions")*. Bridgers cites Elliott Smith as a foundational influence on her sound.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
